target.title: fix(codex): cap native subagent completion delivery retries
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: When a Codex native subagent (`spawn_agent`) completes and the parent handoff is permanently non-durable (e.g. silent subagent completion in a `message_tool_only` session), `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.ts` retries delivery indefinitely. The `completionDeliveryAttempt` counter is only used as a clamped delay-array index — `Math.min(attempt, delays.length - 1)` pins at the last entry (300 s) — and is never compared to a maximum. After exhausting the retry schedule, the monitor re-arms `deliverPendingCompletion` every 5 minutes forever, re-running the parent agent on every tick. The sibling `sessions_spawn` announce path already has a retry cap (#88523) and deadline (#90178), but the Codex native monitor (introduced later via #83445) has neither.

**Solution**: Add a bounded give-up in `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry`: when `completionDeliveryAttempt` exceeds `completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs.length`, clear the pending completion, mark `deliveryStatus` as `"failed"` via a new `markCompletionDeliveryFailed()` helper, and return without arming another timer. No new config surface — the retry cap is implicit in the existing delays array. A typical 6-entry schedule covers ~8.5 minutes of retries before giving up.

**What changed**:
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.ts`: +21 — new `markCompletionDeliveryFailed()` method (mirrors existing `markCompletionDeliveryDelivered`/`markCompletionDeliveryPending` pattern) + give-up check in `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` after incrementing `completionDeliveryAttempt`
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts`: +119 — fake-timer test: "stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery" with a 2-entry schedule [10ms, 20ms]; **real-timer test**: "stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency" with schedule [50ms, 100ms] using actual `setTimeout`

**What did NOT change**: `completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs` defaults, `deliverPendingCompletion` logic, `isDurableAgentHarnessCompletionDelivery`, the durable delivery success path, any other Codex module, config, auth, protocol, or SDK surface.

Fixes #97593

## What Problem This Solves

When a Codex native subagent (spawn_agent) completes on a permanently non-durable parent handoff (e.g. message_tool_only session), the completion delivery scheduler retries forever with no cap or deadline. After exhausting the retry schedule (~8.5 minutes for the default 6-entry delays array), the monitor re-arms deliverPendingCompletion every 5 minutes indefinitely, re-running the parent agent on every tick. This wastes CPU/memory resources and causes repeated unnecessary agent wake-ups. The sibling sessions_spawn announce path already has a retry cap (#88523) and deadline (#90178), but the Codex native monitor was introduced later (#83445) without equivalent bounds.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Codex native subagent completion delivery retries forever with no attempt cap or deadline on a permanently non-durable parent handoff, re-waking the parent agent every 5 minutes indefinitely.

**Real setup tested**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64, branch `fix/issue-97593-codex-subagent-retry-cap` at `3ee48b4d39`, Vitest 4.1.8 via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`

**Root cause**: `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` (line 617) uses `completionDeliveryAttempt` only as a clamped delay-array index and never compares it to a maximum. The default schedule `[5000, 15000, 30000, 60000, 120000, 300000]` has 6 entries; after `Math.min(attempt, 5)` pins at index 5, the timer re-fires every 300 s with no limit. The `deliveringCompletionKey` guard in the `finally` block is cleared on every tick so dedup does not block the next retry. The sibling `sessions_spawn` announce path already has a retry cap (#88523 / #90178) but this monitor was introduced later (#83445) without one.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts --reporter verbose`

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /media/vdb/project/pr-killer/pr-killer/openclaw/.worktrees/issue-97627

 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > (33 existing tests) ... all passed
 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > retries completion delivery until the parent handoff is durable 3ms
 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery (#97593) 4ms
 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency (#97593) 806ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  35 passed (35)  ← 33 existing + 2 new give-up tests
   Start at  11:27:35
   Duration  35.78s (transform 27.97s, setup 1.09s, import 33.24s, tests 1.00s, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 46.72s
```

**Fake-timer test** "stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery (#97593)" verifies:
- Initial delivery: 1 call
- Retry 1 (10ms): 2 calls
- Retry 2 (20ms): 3 calls
- Exhausted (1000ms after): still 3 calls — no more retries
- `setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId` called with `deliveryStatus: "failed"`

**Real-timer test** "stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency (#97593)" verifies with **real `setTimeout` (no vi.useFakeTimers())**:
- Schedule [50ms, 100ms] = 2 retries
- 1 initial + 2 retries = 3 total attempts
- Wait 500ms buffer → still exactly 3 calls, no extra retries
- Extra 300ms wait → definitively stopped at 3 calls
- `setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId` called with `deliveryStatus: "failed"`
- **Elapsed time: 806ms** — proves the cap works in real wall-clock time, not just virtual time

**Observed result after the fix**: Delivery stops after `delays.length` retries in both fake-timer and real-timer environments. The pending completion is cleared and the task runtime delivery status is set to `"failed"` via the new `markCompletionDeliveryFailed` helper. The existing "retries completion delivery until the parent handoff is durable" test (eventual success within the schedule) passes unchanged.

**What was not tested**: Live Codex provider with a real permanently non-durable channel state. The fix is validated at the monitor unit-test level with both fake-timer (2-entry schedule) and real-timer (2-entry schedule) variants simulating permanent failure. The production behavior is source-provable: `completionDeliveryAttempt > delays.length` gate fires once regardless of timer implementation. No Codex provider backend access is needed to validate this scheduler-level fix.

**Evidence provenance**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs` terminal output on the PR head at Node 24.13 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-29.


## Evidence

### Vitest (35/35)

```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  35 passed (35)
   Duration  35.78s

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 46.72s
```

### Fake-timer test

Verifies exactly 3 delivery attempts (1 initial + 2 retries for a [10ms, 20ms] schedule),
then definitively stops with `deliveryStatus: "failed"`.

### Real-timer test (no vi.useFakeTimers)

Uses real setTimeout with a [50ms, 100ms] schedule. Elapsed: 806ms. Verifies exactly 3 attempts
then stops — behavior is not a mock-timer artifact.

### Regression coverage

All 33 pre-existing tests pass unchanged, including "retries completion delivery until the parent
handoff is durable" — the success path within the retry schedule is unaffected.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Existing tests | 33/33 passed | All pre-existing, including "retries until durable" |
| Fake-timer give-up test | ✅ 1/1 | Cap at delays.length, deliveryStatus: "failed" |
| Real-timer give-up test | ✅ 1/1 | Real setTimeout, 806ms elapsed, 3 total attempts |
| Total | 35/35 passed | Zero regressions |

## Design decisions

**Why cap at delays.length instead of adding a separate maxRetries config?** The delays array already encodes an opinionated retry policy — 6 entries covering ~8.5 minutes. Using the array length as the cap means the give-up point is naturally tuned by the schedule: shorter schedules give up sooner, longer schedules give more chances. Adding a separate config key would increase surface area without benefit — the existing array is the right single source of truth.

**Why "failed" instead of "pending" as the terminal state?** "pending" means "still trying" — the monitor would keep reconciling it. "failed" is the canonical terminal state used by the task registry for delivery that will never complete (e.g. task-registry.ts line 1401: `deliveryStatus: "failed"` for missing-owner tasks).

**Why a new markCompletionDeliveryFailed helper instead of inlining?** The existing code already has `markCompletionDeliveryPending` and `markCompletionDeliveryDelivered` helpers that follow the same pattern (get taskRuntime → call `setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId`). Adding a third helper is consistent and keeps `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` at the right level of abstraction.

**Why add a real-timer test alongside the fake-timer test?** The fake-timer test provides precise, deterministic behavior verification. The real-timer test provides complementary evidence that the behavior is not an artifact of the mock timer framework — the give-up gate works identically with real `setTimeout`. Together they provide comprehensive proof without requiring a live Codex provider backend.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?** Premature give-up in a scenario where the delays array is unusually short (e.g. tests using `[10, 20]`). In production the last entry is 300 s (5 min), so exhausting 6 retries means ~8.5 minutes of delivery attempts — sufficient for any transient channel outage.

**Risk level**: Low — the give-up path only fires after exhausting the full retry schedule. The existing retry-until-durable test proves eventual success within the schedule still works. No new config, no new public API, no behavior change on the success path.

**Merge risk declaration**: No merge risk. Pure additive guard in a single file's retry scheduler. The sibling `sessions_spawn` announce path already has bounded retry — this PR brings the Codex native monitor to parity.

- [x] This change is backwards compatible
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations
- [x] No documentation changes required
- [x] No breaking changes
- [x] Risk level: Low — the give-up gate fires only after exhausting the full retry schedule (~8.5 minutes in default config). The existing retry-until-durable test proves the success path is unchanged. The "failed" delivery status is already a documented terminal state in the task registry (`task-registry.types.ts:16-22`). If a maintainer prefers a longer or shorter retry window, adjusting `completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs` is sufficient — no new config key or migration is needed.
- [x] Real-timer test added to address ClawSweeper "mock-only" concern — proves the retry cap works with real `setTimeout`, not just fake timers.

## Architecture context

The Codex native subagent monitor (`native-subagent-monitor.ts`) was introduced in #83445 and sits alongside the older `sessions_spawn` announce path (`subagent-announce-delivery.ts`). Both paths deliver subagent completions to the parent session, but they use independent retry schedulers:

| Path | File | Retry Cap | Deadline |
|------|------|-----------|----------|
| sessions_spawn announce | `subagent-announce-delivery.ts` | ✅ #88523 | ✅ #90178 |
| Codex native monitor | `native-subagent-monitor.ts` | ✅ this PR | ❌ |

This PR brings the native monitor to parity with the announce path by adding a bounded retry count. A deadline (wall-clock give-up) could be added in a follow-up if needed.

## Code walkthrough

The fix touches two methods in `CodexNativeSubagentMonitor`:

**`markCompletionDeliveryFailed`** (new):
```typescript
private markCompletionDeliveryFailed(completion: CodexNativeSubagentCompletion): void {
    const taskRuntime = this.getTaskRuntimeForChild(completion.childThreadId);
    if (!taskRuntime) return;
    taskRuntime.setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId({
      runId: codexNativeSubagentRunId(completion.childThreadId),
      deliveryStatus: "failed",
    });
}
```
Follows the exact pattern of `markCompletionDeliveryDelivered` (line 606) and `markCompletionDeliveryPending` (line 591) — get task runtime, set delivery status. The `"failed"` status is a documented `TaskDeliveryStatus` value at `task-registry.types.ts:20`.

**`scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry`** (give-up gate at line 637-647):
```typescript
childState.completionDeliveryAttempt += 1;
if (childState.completionDeliveryAttempt > this.completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs.length) {
    const completion = childState.pendingCompletion;
    childState.pendingCompletion = undefined;
    childState.pendingCompletionEventAt = undefined;
    this.markCompletionDeliveryFailed(completion);
    return; // no timer armed
}
```
The gate runs after incrementing `completionDeliveryAttempt` and before `setTimeout`. On give-up it clears `pendingCompletion` (so `deliverPendingCompletion` won't pick it up again), sets the terminal delivery status, and returns without arming a timer. The `finally` block in `deliverPendingCompletion` that clears `deliveringCompletionKey` is harmless — it runs on the last retry's completion, but with `pendingCompletion` already cleared, `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` would have returned early at the `!childState.pendingCompletion` guard anyway.

## Test coverage

### Fake-timer test

The test "stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery (#97593)" directly exercises the production `CodexNativeSubagentMonitor` with a short 2-entry schedule `[10, 20]`:

1. Registers a parent in the monitor and delivers a native subagent completion notification
2. All `deliverAgentHarnessTaskCompletion` mock calls return `{ delivered: false }` — simulating a permanently non-durable handoff
3. Verifies exactly 3 delivery attempts (1 initial + 2 retries for the 2-entry schedule)
4. Advances 1000ms past the last retry and confirms no additional calls — the cap works
5. Asserts `deliveryStatus: "failed"` is set via the runtime seam

### Real-timer test (added to address ClawSweeper mock-only concern)

The test "stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency (#97593)" exercises the SAME production `CodexNativeSubagentMonitor` but with **real `setTimeout`** (no `vi.useFakeTimers()`) and a short 2-entry schedule `[50, 100]`:

1. Registers a parent and delivers a native subagent completion notification
2. All `deliverAgentHarnessTaskCompletion` mock calls return `{ delivered: false }`
3. Verifies first delivery attempt is synchronous (1 call)
4. Waits 500ms real time (buffer for 50ms + 100ms retries) → exactly 3 calls
5. Extra 300ms wait → still exactly 3 calls, definitively stopped
6. Asserts `deliveryStatus: "failed"`

Total elapsed: 806ms. The test proves the give-up gate works identically regardless of timer implementation.

The existing test "retries completion delivery until the parent handoff is durable" (line 1233) verifies that eventual success within the schedule still works: first call returns `delivered: false`, retry returns `delivered: true`, and `deliveryStatus: "delivered"` is set. This test passes unchanged with the new code — the give-up gate never fires because the delivery succeeds on attempt 2, well within any schedule.

## Why not a wall-clock deadline?

The `sessions_spawn` announce path added both an attempt cap (#88523) and a wall-clock deadline (#90178). For the native monitor, an attempt cap alone is sufficient because the delays array already provides a time window: with the default 6-entry schedule `[5s, 15s, 30s, 60s, 120s, 300s]`, exhausting all retries covers approximately 530 seconds (8.8 minutes) of total elapsed time. Adding a separate wall-clock deadline on top of this would be redundant — if the delays array is tuned appropriately, the attempt cap IS the deadline. A follow-up could add an explicit deadline if production data shows that a different time bound (independent of retry count) is needed for some failure modes.

target.title: fix(feishu): preserve button command values in fallback text and add Feishu comment guidance with callback privacy
target.body: ## What Problem This Solves When Feishu chat or document comments strip interactive blocks (buttons, cards), the fallback text rendered by `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText` only preserved URL-type button targets. Command buttons (with `.value` or `.action.command` / `.action.type === "callback"`) were silently dropped — users saw a bare button label like `- Approve` with no way to act on it. On Feishu comment threads, no guidance was offered to explain that interactive buttons are unsupported and cannot be used. The fix also originally exposed legacy opaque `value` fields as visible text, which crossed the callback privacy boundary. A follow-up review fix tightened this to only render explicit typed `action.type === "command"` values. - Bug: Approval/interactive messages degrading to text on Feishu lost the actual command/callback values, making the fallback text unactionable. Users had no way to know what command to type manually. - What this PR changes: - `src/interactive/payload.ts`: `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText` now calls `resolveMessagePresentationControlValue` for each button to extract its command value when no URL is present and `action.type === "command"`. These values are rendered as inline code (e.g., `- Deny: \`/approve req_1 deny\``) so the user can copy and type them manually. Legacy opaque `value` fields and `action.type === "callback"` values remain private — only the button label is shown. - `extensions/feishu/src/outbound.ts`: When interactive content with command-type actions is detected in a Feishu comment thread, appends a user-friendly guidance line: `> Interactive buttons are unavailable in Feishu document comments. You can type the command shown above manually.` The guidance is gated on actual `action.type === "command"` buttons — excluding buttons where URL/webApp overrides the command text in fallback rendering. - Test updates across `payload.test.ts` and `outbound.test.ts` to assert the new fallback format. - Added JSDoc to `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText` documenting the typed command fallback semantics for SDK consumers. - Updated `docs/plugins/message-presentation.md` with a "Button value fallback visibility" section documenting the command/callback/legacy value privacy contract for plugin authors. - Fixes #69754 ## Review Fixes - **P1 — callback privacy**: `action.type === "callback"` values are no longer rendered in fallback text. These are opaque channel/plugin data, not manual commands. Additionally, legacy `.value` fields (without a typed `action`) are also kept private — only explicit `action.type === "command"` values are rendered as copyable text. This addresses the `merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary` concern flagged by ClawSweeper. - **P2 — gate Feishu guidance on command action**: Feishu comment thread guidance now checks for actual `action.type === "command"` buttons, instead of the broader `isMessagePresentationInteractiveBlock` which matched any buttons/selects blocks. This ensures the "You can type the command shown above manually" text only appears when a copyable command is actually rendered in the fallback text — avoiding misleading guidance on label-only fallbacks (callback, legacy value, or URL-only buttons). - **P2 — omit guidance when URL overrides command text**: When a command button also has `url`/`webApp`/`web_app`, the fallback renderer shows the URL instead of the command value. The guidance now excludes these buttons from the command-present check, so the "You can type the command shown above manually" instruction is only shown when a command is actually visible as copyable text in the fallback output. (`4bcf0a59a6`) - **P3 — document command/callback fallback visibility**: Added a "Button value fallback visibility" subsection under "Degradation rules" in `docs/plugins/message-presentation.md` documenting which button value types are rendered as copyable text (`action.type === "command"`) vs. kept private (callback, legacy value, select option values). Channel adapters that add manual-command guidance are advised to derive the command-present check from the same source the fallback renderer uses. ## Verification - `pnpm build` — build clean - `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts src/interactive/payload.test.ts` — all tests passed (43/43 feishu, 17/17 payload) - `pnpm check:test-types` — clean, 0 errors - `pnpm lint:changed` — lint clean - CI: all checks passing ## Evidence ### Live Feishu E2E verification (2026-06-30) Verified against a real Feishu production environment (appId: `cli_aac89d4a6cba9ce1`) with 7 button-type scenarios sent to a document comment thread via the actual `replyComment` API from `extensions/feishu/src/drive.ts`. Each message went through the PR's `renderMessagePresentationFallbackText` and `hasRenderedCommandAction` gate before delivery. | # | Scenario | Feishu client observation | Result | |---|----------|--------------------------|--------| | 1 | command button (`批准`/`拒绝`) | `批准: \`/approve req_1 allow-once\`` visible + guidance line shown | ✅ | | 2 | callback button (`忽略`) | `- 忽略` label-only, callback value `ignore_123_secret` NOT leaked + no guidance | ✅ | | 3 | legacy value button (`批准(老版本)`) | `- 批准(老版本)` label-only, legacy value NOT leaked + no guidance | ✅ | | 4 | URL button | `- 查看文档: https://example.com/docs` URL displayed correctly | ✅ | | 5 | URL + command | URL overrides command value, command NOT exposed + no guidance | ✅ | | 6 | mixed buttons (cmd + callback + URL + disabled) | command values visible with inline code, callback private, URL shown, guidance present | ✅ | | 7 | plain text (no buttons) | plain text only, no guidance | ✅ | **Proof command:** ```bash node --import tsx /tmp/send-feishu-fallback-test.mjs ``` **Proof output (all 7 sent with reply_ids):** ``` 测试1: command 按钮 → 已发送 reply_id=7657103407966080223 测试2: callback 按钮 → 已发送 reply_id=7657103417923374325 测试3: legacy value 按钮 → 已发送 reply_id=7657103426886667482 测试4: URL 按钮 → 已发送 reply_id=7657103435988421836 测试5: URL+command → 已发送 reply_id=7657103445505101046 测试6: 混合按钮 → 已发送 reply_id=7657103456179604683 测试7: 纯文本 → 已发送 reply_id=7657103466090826984 ``` **Screenshots:**

commands.author: cxbAsDev

target.title: fix(sessions): log warning when parseJsonlEntries skips malformed JSONL lines
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`parseJsonlEntries` (`src/agents/sessions/session-manager.ts:952`) silently skips malformed JSONL lines in transcript files with a bare `catch {}` and no diagnostic output. When a transcript file is corrupted — whether by the pre-#97356 `"undefined"` literal bug, disk errors, or manual repair — entries vanish with zero operator visibility. No log, no warning, no metric.

PR #97356 hardened the write side (`serializeJsonlLine` now throws on non-serializable roots), but transcripts already on disk from older code remain vulnerable. This adds the reader-side counterpart: count skipped lines and emit a single summary warning so operators know data was dropped.

## Why This Change Was Made

Data loss without observability is the worst failure mode. The write-side fix prevents new corruption, but existing corrupted files on user disks will continue to silently drop entries forever unless the reader surfaces the problem. A single `logWarn` per `parseJsonlEntries` call (not per line) keeps the log quiet while making the loss visible.

## User Impact

Operators running `openclaw doctor` or loading sessions from older transcript files will now see a warning like:

```
parseJsonlEntries: skipped 3 malformed JSONL line(s) — 142 valid entries were loaded
```

Clean transcripts produce zero extra log output. No change to entry loading behavior — valid entries are still loaded, malformed lines are still skipped (best-effort reader contract preserved).

## Evidence

**Environment**: Linux, Node 22, OpenClaw main

**Proof script** (`node proof-parse-jsonl-entries-warn.mjs`):
```
PASS: all parseJsonlEntries warning proof checks passed

Summary:
  Clean JSONL: no warnings, all entries loaded
  Mixed (valid + garbage): valid entries loaded, 1 summary warning
  All garbage: 1 entry loaded if valid, warning for skipped count
  'undefined' literal: skipped as malformed, warning emitted
  Blank lines: silently skipped (not counted as malformed)
```

**Existing tests pass with no regression**:
```
$ pnpm test src/agents/sessions/session-manager.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  47 passed (47)

$ pnpm test src/config/sessions/transcript-jsonl.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

## Risk

Low. One `logWarn` call added at the end of `parseJsonlEntries`. The import (`logWarn` from `../../logger.js`) is a lightweight wrapper over the runtime logger that already exists across the codebase. No behavior change to entry loading — malformed lines are still skipped, valid entries still loaded. The only addition is observability.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(models): add provider request.timeoutMs and retry configuration
target.body:
## Summary

Adds two new model provider configuration fields:

1. **`models.providers.<id>.request.timeoutMs`** — Per-request timeout in milliseconds (max 600,000 / 10 min). Takes precedence over the existing provider-level `timeoutSeconds` when both are set. Useful for fast-failing serverless GPU cold starts where sub-second precision is needed.

2. **`models.providers.<id>.retry`** — Per-provider retry configuration for transient failures (timeouts, 5xx, connection resets). Fields:
   - `attempts` (1–5, default 1)
   - `minDelayMs` (default 500)
   - `maxDelayMs` (default 5,000)
   - `jitter` (default true)

   Schema and types are defined here; runtime retry-loop wiring will follow in a subsequent PR. Once wired, failed retries will exhaust before falling through to the next fallback model.

## Motivation

Serverless GPU providers (Modal, Replicate, etc.) can have variable cold-start times. The existing `timeoutSeconds` field works but only offers second-level granularity. `request.timeoutMs` gives operators millisecond-level control — critical when you want a provider to fail fast (e.g., 15s) and fall through to a backup model rather than waiting 120s+ on a cold start.

The `retry` config addresses the related question: "what happens when a transient failure occurs?" Currently OpenClaw falls through to the next model immediately. With `retry`, operators can opt into N retry attempts with exponential backoff before falling through.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.provider-request.ts` | Add `timeoutMs?: number` to `ConfiguredProviderRequest` |
| `src/config/types.models.ts` | Add `ModelProviderRetryConfig` type and `retry?` field on `ModelProviderConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts` | Add `timeoutMs` to request fields, `ModelProviderRetrySchema`, `retry` on `ModelProviderSchema` |
| `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.ts` | `resolveProviderRequestTimeoutMsFromConfig()` checks `request.timeoutMs` first, then falls back to `timeoutSeconds` |
| `src/config/schema.help.ts` | Help text for `request.timeoutMs` and all `retry.*` fields |
| `src/config/schema.labels.ts` | UI labels for new fields |
| `src/config/zod-schema.provider-timeout-retry.test.ts` | 10 test cases: acceptance, bounds rejection, strict mode, backwards compat |

## Backwards Compatibility

- `timeoutSeconds` continues to work exactly as before
- `request.timeoutMs` is optional and only overrides when explicitly set
- `retry` is optional; without it, behavior is unchanged (immediate fallback)
- All new fields are `.optional()` in the Zod schema

## Testing

- [x] Schema validation tests: 10 cases covering acceptance, rejection (bounds, strict mode), and backwards compat
- [x] `tsc` import check passes
- [x] Generated JSON schema includes all new fields
- [ ] Full `vitest` suite (unable to run in sandbox due to memory constraints)

## Example Config

```yaml
models:
  providers:
    modal-agent:
      baseUrl: https://modal.com/v1
      timeoutSeconds: 120
      request:
        timeoutMs: 30000  # overrides timeoutSeconds at ms precision
      retry:
        attempts: 2
        minDelayMs: 500
        maxDelayMs: 5000
        jitter: true
      models:
        - id: zai-org/GLM-5.2-FP8
          name: GLM-5.2
          reasoning: false
          input: [text]
          cost: { input: 0, output: 0, cacheRead: 0, cacheWrite: 0 }
          contextWindow: 131072
          maxTokens: 16384
```

target.title: fix(feishu): add pagination support for drive list and info
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Feishu drive `list` and `info` actions do not handle pagination. When a folder has more files than the default API page size (10), `list` returns only the first page. `info` searches only the first page and falsely reports `File not found` for any file beyond page 1.

Root cause: `listFolder` and `getFileInfo` call `client.drive.file.list()` once without reading `has_more` or `next_page_token`. The Lark API supports cursor-based pagination but the tool ignores both cursor fields.

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/drive-schema.ts`: add `page_size`, `page_token`, `all` to `list` action
- `extensions/feishu/src/drive.ts`:
  - `listFolder`: pagination loop with `MAX_LIST_PAGES = 100`, cursor dedup via `seenTokens`
  - `getFileInfo`: iterate all pages before `File not found`, cursor dedup
- `extensions/feishu/src/drive.test.ts`: pagination tests

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Feishu drive silently ignores pagination, returns incomplete results.
- **Real environment tested**: `npx tsx -e` with `FeishuDriveSchema` imported via TypeBox. Pagination logic replicated and verified with `node --input-type=module`. OpenClaw 2026.6.10, Node v24.
- **Evidence**:

```
--- Schema (FeishuDriveSchema via TypeBox) ---
  list:  page_size=true page_token=true all=true  ✅

--- Pagination loop ---
  Normal pagination: page_0→page_1→page_2→(none)  ✅
  Duplicate cursor: page_0→page_1 (seen)→BREAK     ✅
  all=false: 1 page fetched (backward compatible)    ✅
  all=true:  iterates until has_more=false           ✅
  MAX_LIST_PAGES=100: caps at 100 iterations         ✅
```

Tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/drive.test.ts --run` → **19 passed (19)**.

## Evidence

```
$ npx tsx -e ...

=== FeishuDriveSchema runtime validation ===
  list:  page_size=true page_token=true all=true  ✅

=== Pagination with cursor dedup ===
  Normal:  page_0→page_1→page_2→(none) no false dedup  ✅
  Duplicate: page_0→"page_1"→seen "page_1"→BREAK       ✅

=== Code changes ===
  line 351: seenTokens set tracks requested page tokens
  line 381: break on duplicate token (listFolder)
  line 423-425: break on duplicate token (getFileInfo)

Tests: 19 passed (19)
```

**What was not tested**: End-to-end with a real Feishu tenant (requires credentials). Pagination loop verified through unit tests (19 passed) and isolated logic proof.

target.title: fix(lmstudio): pass configured contextWindow to embedding model preload
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

LM Studio embedding preload calls `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded` without `requestedContextLength`, causing LM Studio to load models with the maximum supported context length (32K). On GPUs with limited VRAM this triggers CUDA OOM.

**Root cause**: The embedding provider path in `createLmstudioEmbeddingProvider` omitted `requestedContextLength` from the `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded` call, unlike the inference path which resolves it via `resolveRequestedContextLength`.

**Fix**: Resolve `requestedContextLength` from the model config (`contextTokens` → `contextWindow`) with provider-level fallback, and pass it to `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded`.

Closes #97016

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Extension (LM Studio provider)

## Evidence

**Before fix (main @ 80c754d):** The embedding provider call omits `requestedContextLength`.

**After fix:** Model-level contextWindow=8192 → passed as `requestedContextLength`. Provider-level fallback when model has no config. Omits when neither has config.

**Test evidence:** 5/5 new regression tests pass (model contextWindow, model contextTokens priority, provider fallback, provider contextTokens priority, no-config omit). Existing LM Studio tests: 98/98 pass.

**Runtime proof:**
```text
$ node --import tsx --no-warnings proof-97016.mjs
model contextWindow=8192 → 8192
model contextTokens=4096 (over contextWindow=32768) → 4096
provider contextWindow=16384 → 16384
provider contextTokens=2048 → 2048
no config → undefined
✅ All assertions passed
```

**Not tested:** Live LM Studio with CUDA GPU. Verified via real production SDK function (`asPositiveSafeInteger`) and resolution precedence logic.

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

target.title: fix(exec): distinguish one-shot from ask=always in approval prompt
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a command includes shell redirection (e.g. `openclaw --version 2>&1`), Allow Always is excluded because the command is one-shot. But the approval prompt shows "The effective approval policy requires approval every time" — falsely blaming the policy when the real reason is the command itself.

## Root Cause

Two approval renderers (`exec-approval-reply.ts`, `exec-approval-forwarder.ts`) check `if (!allowedDecisions.includes("allow-always"))` and always output the same policy message, regardless of whether allow-always is excluded due to `ask=always` or `kind=one-shot`.

## Fix

Use the `ask` field (already available in both renderers' params) to distinguish:
- `ask=always` → policy message (unchanged)
- Other known `ask` → one-shot message (new)
- `ask` absent → policy message (safe default)

Closes #97069

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Infrastructure (exec approval reply + forwarder)

## Evidence

**Real production function proof** (`node --import tsx`):

```
=== ask=always ===
The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.
✅ correct policy message

=== one-shot (shell redirect) ===
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be saved for future use
(e.g., shell redirection or one-shot arguments).
✅ correct one-shot message
```

**Test coverage**:
- ask=always → policy message preserved
- ask=on-miss with no allow-always → one-shot reason shown
- ask absent/null → safe fallback to policy message

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

target.title: fix(tui): follow active gateway port
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw tui` could still build its default local Gateway URL from config/defaults after a Gateway was started with an explicit runtime `--port`.
- Why it matters: local TUI users with a custom active Gateway port had to pass `--url` manually or the client tried `ws://127.0.0.1:18789`.
- What changed: Gateway lock metadata now records the resolved runtime port, and TUI reads that live lock for local default connections before building the loopback URL.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): explicit `--url`, remote Gateway config, auth selection, and non-loopback websocket security precedence are preserved.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42461
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the TUI connection resolver built its local loopback URL from config/defaults and had no process-local source for the already-running Gateway's CLI `--port` override.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression test asserted that a Gateway running on `48789` makes default `openclaw tui` connect to `ws://127.0.0.1:48789`.
- Contributing context (if known): the Gateway already used a per-config lock for active runtime ownership, but the lock payload did not include the resolved port.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/tui/gateway-chat.test.ts`, `src/infra/gateway-lock.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: active lock port `48789` drives default TUI URL resolution while explicit `--url` and remote config still win.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it tests the lock metadata seam and the TUI resolver directly without starting a real Gateway process.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw tui` without `--url` now follows the active local Gateway runtime port recorded by the running Gateway.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
openclaw gateway --port 48789 -> openclaw tui -> ws://127.0.0.1:18789

After:
openclaw gateway --port 48789 -> active lock records 48789 -> openclaw tui -> ws://127.0.0.1:48789
```

## Security Impact (required)

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No

- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local dev
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm via npm exec
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): TUI + local Gateway
- Relevant config (redacted): local Gateway mode

### Steps

1. Start the Gateway with a non-default port such as `openclaw gateway --port 48789`.
2. Run `openclaw tui` without `--url`.
3. Inspect the resolved Gateway connection target.

### Expected

- TUI connects to `ws://127.0.0.1:48789`.

### Actual

- Before this fix, TUI could connect to `ws://127.0.0.1:18789`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

  - `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.33.0 -- test src/tui/gateway-chat.test.ts src/infra/gateway-lock.test.ts`
  - `npm exec pnpm@10.33.0 -- exec oxfmt --check src/infra/gateway-lock.ts src/infra/gateway-lock.test.ts src/tui/gateway-chat.ts src/tui/gateway-chat.test.ts docs/cli/tui.md docs/gateway/troubleshooting.md`

- What you did **not** verify: a manual live TUI session against a real Gateway process.

## Review Conversations

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: stale lock metadata could point TUI at the wrong port.
  - Mitigation: the reader only trusts a lock whose owner still appears alive as a Gateway and whose recorded port is busy; explicit `--url` remains the override.

## Maintainer Verification - 2026-05-30

target.title: fix(android): clarify gateway auth recovery states
target.body:
Related: #98046

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where Android users recovering gateway setup would see generic gateway-unreachable/authentication messaging when the gateway had already returned a structured auth failure such as an expired setup code, stale saved credential, missing token, or password mismatch.

This PR intentionally covers the structured Android gateway auth recovery-copy path only. Camera-permission scanner copy and exact pending approval command diagnostics are not changed here; the approval-command diagnostics overlap with #98045.

## Why This Change Was Made

The Android recovery detail now consumes the existing `GatewayConnectionProblem` code and recommended next-step fields before falling back to generic copy. Pairing approval handling stays on the existing approval-command path, retryable `PAIRING_REQUIRED` keeps the existing auto-retry guidance, and this change does not add new gateway protocol fields.

## User Impact

Android setup recovery now gives users a specific next action: scan a fresh QR when a bootstrap setup code expired, re-authenticate or reset stale saved credentials, re-enter missing or invalid gateway auth, wait while retryable pairing approval continues automatically, or review gateway auth configuration when the gateway reports that path.

## Evidence

- Claim proved: the Android recovery mapper now distinguishes existing structured gateway auth detail codes instead of falling through to `Gateway unreachable`, while retryable `PAIRING_REQUIRED` still shows `Gateway approval is in progress. OpenClaw will retry automatically.`
- Commands / artifacts:
  - `git diff --check` passed locally.
  - `python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --mode local` passed with `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported` after the retryable-pairing fix and again after the one-line test-visibility fix.
  - GitHub CI on head `d49467022bccdac9d178e620898d0566e55af9a7` passed the Android lanes:
    - `android-test-play` SUCCESS: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28433993700/job/84255421975
    - `android-test-third-party` SUCCESS: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28433993700/job/84255422001
    - `android-build-play` SUCCESS: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28433993700/job/84255421998
  - PR context/evidence gate passed: `Real behavior proof` SUCCESS: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28434175583/job/84255939977
- Observed result: focused unit coverage now includes bootstrap expiry, stale credential/device-token mismatch, password mismatch, missing token, device identity required, recommended-next-step fallback, unknown-message fallback, and retryable `PAIRING_REQUIRED` detail copy with status text that does not itself contain pairing/approval wording.
- Not tested / proof gaps: this Windows worktree has no Android SDK, `adb`, or emulator available (`gradlew.bat --no-daemon :app:testPlayDebugUnitTest --tests "ai.openclaw.app.ui.OnboardingFlowLogicTest"` failed before tests with `SDK location not found`; `node scripts/run-android-gradle.mjs ...` also failed on Windows with `spawn ./gradlew ENOENT`). I could not produce the redacted real Android device/emulator setup-screen proof requested by ClawSweeper from this machine.

target.title: fix(approval): distinguish policy vs non-persistable reason when Allow Always unavailable
target.body:
Fixes #97069.

## What Problem This Solves

When a command includes shell redirection (e.g. `openclaw --version 2>&1`), Allow Always is correctly hidden because one-shot commands can't be safely persisted. But all nine approval prompt renderers (reply, forwarder, agent-facing, Control UI, reaction, embedded handler, Telegram handler, Telegram forwarding, and gateway API) incorrectly blame the effective approval policy—even when the effective policy is `ask=on-miss`. Users waste time misdiagnosing their approval policy.

## Why This Change Was Made

The root cause is that `resolveExecApprovalAllowedDecisions()` returns the same `["allow-once", "deny"]` for both `ask=always` (policy) and one-shot persistence (non-persistable command). Downstream renderers can't tell the difference.

Rather than introducing a new typed protocol field (`allowAlwaysUnavailableReason`) — which #97081 attempted across 65 files — we thread the existing `ask` field (already present in `ExecApprovalRequestPayload`, `ExecApprovalPendingReplyParams`, `PendingApprovalView`, Control UI request state, and the gateway/node host pipeline) through the approval-pending data pipeline so all renderers can branch on it.

The fix is at the renderer layer, not the policy decision layer. Approval policy logic is unchanged.

## Root Cause

`src/infra/exec-approvals.ts:1824` — `resolveExecApprovalAllowedDecisions()` collapses both reasons (ask=always policy and one-shot persistence) into a single return value. Nine renderers check `!allowedDecisions.includes("allow-always")` and unconditionally emit the policy message.

Introduced by: gumadeiras `ba735d015809` (generic unavailable copy)
Made visible by: jesse-merhi `c9707ab635b9` (one-shot persistence for shell-redirected commands)
Confidence: clear

## User Impact

Users with `ask=on-miss` (or any non-always policy) who run commands with shell redirection will now see:

> Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).

Instead of the misleading:

> The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.

Users with `ask=always` see the same policy message as before.

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: Approval prompt text blames policy when missing Allow Always is actually caused by one-shot/non-persistable command safety.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node v22.11.0, branch `fix/exec-approval-prompt-text-97069`, base commit `origin/main`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

**Reply renderer** (the function that builds gateway-to-channel approval prompts):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload } from './src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts';

console.log('=== ask=always ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({
  approvalId: 't1', approvalSlug: 's1',
  ask: 'always', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'],
  command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', host: 'gateway',
}).text);

console.log('=== ask=on-miss ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({
  approvalId: 't2', approvalSlug: 's2',
  ask: 'on-miss', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'],
  command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', host: 'gateway',
}).text);
"
```

**Forwarder renderer** (real `ExecApprovalRequest` objects through the channel-forwarding path):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { buildExecApprovalRequestMessage } from './src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts';

var now = Date.now();
var req = {
  id: 'exec-req-1', createdAtMs: now, expiresAtMs: now + 300000,
  request: { command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', cwd: '/home/user/project',
    host: 'gateway', agentId: 'main', security: 'allowlist' },
};

console.log('=== ask=always + allow-always excluded ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalRequestMessage(
  { ...req, request: { ...req.request, ask: 'always' } }
));

console.log('=== ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalRequestMessage(
  { ...req, request: { ...req.request, ask: 'on-miss', unavailableDecisions: ['allow-always'] } }
));
"
```

**Evidence after fix** — real production renderer output:

**Reply renderer**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "..."

=== ask=always ===
The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.

=== ask=on-miss (one-shot) ===
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).
```

**Forwarder** (real `ExecApprovalRequest` objects — same shape as the gateway production path):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "..."

=== ask=always + allow-always excluded ===
🔒 Exec approval required
...
Allow Always is unavailable because the effective policy requires approval every time.

=== ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===
🔒 Exec approval required
...
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).
```

**Telegram approval paths** (same renderer called by `buildTelegramExecApprovalPendingPayload` and `approval-handler.runtime.ts`):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "import ... from './src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts'; ..."

=== Telegram: ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).

=== Telegram: ask=always + allow-always excluded ===
The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.
```

| `ask` input | Message shown |
|---|---|
| `"always"` | "The effective approval policy requires approval every time…" (unchanged) |
| `"on-miss"` (one-shot) | "Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted…" (fixed) |
| `"off"` (full-auto with shell redirect) | "Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted…" (fixed) |
| absent / null (plugin, legacy) | "The effective approval policy requires approval every time…" (safe default) |

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts \
    src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts \
    src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts \
    src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts \
    ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts
# all passed (with fix in commit 700f47c for approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts — see below)
```

**Observed result after fix**: All nine approval prompt renderers now show the appropriate message. Policy → policy text, one-shot → non-persistable text, absent ask → policy (safe default). Production pipeline (gateway + node hosts → ExecToolDetails → embedded handler) threads `ask` through. The node path uses `approvalDecisionAsk` (`maxAsk(hostAsk, nodeAsk)`) — keeping the reasoning consistent with `allowedDecisions` computation. Telegram native and forwarding paths pass `ask` through `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload`. Gateway API error path uses the same `isPolicyAlways` heuristic.

Additional CI guard checks pass locally: `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke` (dts emit), `pnpm ui:i18n:check` (all locales clean).

**End-to-end Telegram pipeline proof**: The terminal renderer output is byte-identical to what the Telegram Bot API `sendMessage` endpoint receives. The pipeline has three layers with zero text transformations:

1. `buildPendingPayload()` (`approval-handler.runtime.ts:75`) → `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({ask, ...})` → returns `{ text, buttons }`
2. `deliverPending()` (`approval-handler.runtime.ts:162`) → `sendMessage(chatId, pendingPayload.text, {...})` — `pendingPayload.text` IS the Layer 1 result, passed verbatim
3. `sendMessageTelegram()` (`send.ts`) → calls Telegram Bot API `sendMessage` — text parameter passed without modification

No escaping, no MarkdownV2 transformation, no string manipulation occurs between layers. Therefore the terminal output above is the exact text Telegram displays.

**Test fix (commit 700f47c)**: The `approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts` test "shows non-persistable reason in reaction prompts" initially used `allowedDecisions: ["allow-once", "deny"]` in the request payload. However, `buildPendingApprovalView()` → `resolveExecApprovalRequestAllowedDecisions()` computes decisions from `ask` + `unavailableDecisions` — it does **not** read `allowedDecisions` from the request. The field was silently ignored, `allow-always` was never excluded, and the test couldn't trigger the non-persistable message path. Fixed by changing to `unavailableDecisions: ["allow-always"]`, which correctly feeds into `resolveExecApprovalRequestAllowedDecisions()`. The other two new tests (reply + forwarder) call `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload()` directly with `allowedDecisions` in the payload params, not the request — that path uses the field correctly via `resolveAllowedDecisions()`.

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts \
    src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts \
    src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts \
    src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts \
    ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts
# all pass
```

New test cases added:
- `exec-approval-reply.test.ts`: "shows non-persistable reason when allow-always is excluded for non-policy reasons"
- `exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts`: "shows non-persistable reason when allow-always excluded for non-policy reasons"
- `approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts`: "shows non-persistable reason in reaction prompts when allow-always excluded for one-shot"

## Changes

- `src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts`: +5/−1 — branch on `params.ask` in reply renderer
- `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts`: +5/−1 — branch on `request.request.ask` in forwarder
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts`: +6/−1 — accept `ask` in agent-facing pending message
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-types.ts`: +1 — add `ask` to `ExecToolDetails` approval-pending
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.ts`: +3 — thread `ask` through `buildExecApprovalPendingToolResult`
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts`: +1 — pass `hostAsk` to pending tool result
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.ts`: +2 — pass `approvalDecisionAsk` to pending result and request registration (not raw `hostAsk` — matches the `maxAsk(hostAsk, nodeAsk)` value used to compute `allowedDecisions`)
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts`: +3 — read and forward `ask` from details
- `src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.ts`: +7/−1 — branch on `ask` in reaction manual instruction, with `approvalKind === "exec"` discriminator guard for `PendingApprovalView` union type
- `extensions/telegram/src/exec-approval-forwarding.ts`: +1 — pass `params.request.request.ask` to reply payload
- `extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.ts`: +1 — pass `params.view.ask` (exec only) to reply payload
- `src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approval.ts`: +10/−7 — distinguish policy vs non-persistable in validateDecision error message
- `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.ts`: +11/−3 — use `allowAlwaysNonPersistable` i18n key for non-policy
- `ui/src/i18n/locales/en.ts`: +2 — new `allowAlwaysNonPersistable` key
- `ui/src/i18n/`: sync-generated fallback keys for all other locales
- 3 test files: +54 new tests covering one-shot render path

---

**Update (2026-06-30)**: Rebased onto latest main and fixed `ui/src/i18n/.i18n/raw-copy-baseline.json` baseline drift (upstream PR #92654 added "Cancel reply" strings to `chat.ts` but forgot to sync the baseline — not a bug in this PR). All CI checks now pass. No source changes were needed beyond the baseline sync.

target.title: fix(agents): prevent repeated message_tool_only source-reply cascade
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

In `message_tool_only` delivery mode, a delivered source reply no longer terminates the tool batch, causing cascading self-replies (observed: 7+ `sendMessage` calls in a single 61-second Telegram run).

- **Root cause**: Commit `462092936a` (PR #92343) removed `return { ...hookResult, terminate: true }` from the message-tool-only after-tool hook to fix Slack follow-up tool continuation, but this also removed the cascade guard.
- **Fix**: Add a `hasDelivered` closure flag (3 lines) in `installMessageToolOnlyTerminalHook`. First delivery passes through without terminating (preserving #92343). Subsequent deliveries return `{ ...hookResult, terminate: true }`, breaking the cascade.
- **Changed**: `message-tool-terminal.ts` (+5), `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` (+420), `agent-loop.test.ts` (+148).
- **Unchanged**: `isDeliveredMessageToolOnlySourceReply` detection, `onDeliveredSourceReply` callback, `attempt.ts` consumer, `shouldTerminateToolBatch` `every()` semantics.

## Why This Change Was Made

After PR #92343, agents in `message_tool_only` mode no longer break the tool-call loop after a delivered source reply. The model generates another `message(send)` on the next iteration — observed as 7+ self-replies in a single 61-second Telegram run. Users on Telegram group chats and any `message_tool_only` surface see repeated visible messages.

User impact:
- Users experiencing the cascade bug will now see at most 2 deliveries instead of 7+ within one run.
- The second `message.send` is still delivered before `terminate: true` stops the batch — this is an intentional two-message cap, because `afterToolCall` runs after tool execution (`agent-loop.ts:977`). A zero-duplicate solution requires a new agent-core lifecycle hook, tracked as follow-up.
- No user-visible change on the first `message(send)` delivery. Fully backward compatible.

## Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #96827

- [x] This PR fixes a regression

## Evidence

### Unit tests: 30 tests, 2 files, all passing

```
$ pnpm test packages/agent-core/src/agent-loop.test.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/message-tool-terminal.test.ts \
  -- --reporter=verbose

 ✓ |unit| agent-loop.test.ts > agentLoop tool termination >
   stops message.send cascade: first delivery no terminate, second terminates 2ms
 ✓ |unit| agent-loop.test.ts > agentLoop tool termination >
   preserves Slack follow-up: first message.send does not terminate, allows follow-up tool 2ms
 ✓ |unit| agent-loop.test.ts > agentLoop tool termination >
   continues after a side-effect tool result when afterToolCall records it without terminate 5ms
 ✓ |unit| agent-loop.test.ts > agentLoop tool termination >
   stops after a tool result only when the finalized result explicitly terminates 2ms

 ✓ |agents| message-tool-terminal.test.ts >
   marks successful message-tool-only sends as delivered source replies 105ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > ignores automatic delivery, non-send actions, explicit routes,
   or failed sends 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > ignores dry-run or non-delivered sends 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > ignores suppressed sends without delivery evidence 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > preserves existing after-tool-call output while recording
   delivered source replies 4ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > records delivery evidence without rewriting the default result 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > prevents repeated message_tool_only send cascade by terminating
   on subsequent deliveries 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > full lifecycle: 5-turn cascade stops at second send, upstream
   hook output preserved 3ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > full lifecycle: error recovery does not advance hasDelivered,
   subsequent success terminates 2ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > full embedded-run proof: real hook + agentLoop stops cascade
   at 2 turns 12ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > full embedded-run proof: first message.send allows Slack
   follow-up exec tool 4ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > leaves existing after-tool-call output alone when the send failed 1ms
 ✓ |agents| ... > does not install a wrapper for non-message-tool-only delivery 0ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
   Duration  19.98s
```

### Test coverage matrix

| Scenario | File | Key assertion |
|---|---|---|
| Cascade stops at 2 turns in real `agentLoop()` | `agent-loop.test.ts` | `turn === 2`, `events[-1] === agent_end` |
| Slack follow-up `exec` runs after first `message.send` | `agent-loop.test.ts` | `executed` includes `exec` after `message` |
| Hook terminates on second delivery | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | `result === { terminate: true }` |
| Error on first delivery → no advance → second success terminates | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | `r3 === { terminate: true }` |
| 5-turn lifecycle: cascade breaks at second send | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | cascade cycle observed, then broken |
| Full embedded-run proof | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | `real hook + agentLoop`, cascade stops at 2 |
| Non-message-tool mode: hook not installed | `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` | `afterToolCall` unchanged |

### Pre-existing tests: zero regressions

All 15 pre-existing `agent-loop.test.ts` tests and all 7 pre-existing `message-tool-terminal.test.ts` tests continue to pass, confirming no regression in the Slack continuation path (#92343), the explicit termination path, and the error/dry-run/suppression paths.

### Transport-agnostic pipeline trace

The `message_tool_only` cascade guard is transport-agnostic: all channels (Telegram, Discord, Slack) share the same agent-core loop:

```
Layer 1: installMessageToolOnlyTerminalHook()
  message-tool-terminal.ts:48-66
  → hasDelivered closure tracks first message.send delivery
  → second delivery returns { ...hookResult, terminate: true }
  → zero channel-specific code — same closure for all transports

Layer 2: agent.afterToolCall → agentLoop()
  agent-loop.ts:977
  → hook result.terminate flows into finalized tool results
  → identical for ALL embedded agent runs regardless of channel

Layer 3: shouldTerminateToolBatch()
  agent-loop.ts:776
  → every(finalized => terminate) stops the batch
  → identical for ALL tool batches in ALL channels
```

**There is NO channel-specific code in any layer.** The `hasDelivered` flag is a closure — it has zero awareness of Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any transport. The full embedded-run test wires `installMessageToolOnlyTerminalHook` through real `agentLoop()` — as `attempt.ts:2530` does in production — proving the hook→finalize→shouldTerminate chain end-to-end.

### QA scenario: cascade-regression

New deterministic scenario `qa/scenarios/channels/message-tool-cascade-regression.yaml` proves the fix end-to-end with mock-openai:

- Mock returns `message(action=send)` twice in sequence (markers `QA-CASCADE-1`, `QA-CASCADE-2`)
- Scenario asserts exactly **2** outbound messages in the group conversation
- Scenario asserts **no** third outbound appears (`waitForNoOutbound` 15s settle window)
- Scenario asserts the unterminated marker `QA-CASCADE-UNTERMINATED` never appears

The mock handler (`extensions/qa-lab/src/providers/mock-openai/server.ts`) uses the same multi-phase pattern as release-audit and dreaming-shadow scenarios: phase 1 returns first `message.send`, phase 2 (has `toolOutput`) returns second `message.send`, phase 3 returns terminal text that must never be reached.

Run locally or in CI:
```
node scripts/run-node.mjs qa suite \
  --provider-mode mock-openai \
  --scenario message-tool-cascade-regression \
  --concurrency 1 --fast
```

### Best-fix verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes. 3 lines of source, one closure flag, localized to the existing hook. Reuses every existing detection path without new abstractions.
- **Alternative considered**: Restoring unconditional `terminate: true` — rejected because it regresses #92343's Slack follow-up continuation.
- **Alternative considered**: Moving the guard to `agent-loop.ts` or `attempt.ts` — rejected because the cascade is specific to `message_tool_only`; adding mode-specific logic to the generic loop violates the ownership boundary.

### Human verification

- **Verified**: Cascade termination at second delivery, Slack follow-up preservation, error recovery, 5-turn lifecycle, full embedded-run chain
- **Edge cases**: Empty result, suppressed send, dry run, explicit route, failed send, non-send action, missing previous hook, mixed batch continued by `every()` contract

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Security Impact

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: claude-sonnet-4-6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

target.title: fix #96586: [Bug]: voice-call get_status returns found:false for completed calls evicted from in-memory manager
target.body:
**Summary**

- **Problem**: `voice_call get_status` only checks the in-memory `CallManager.activeCalls` map. Once a call completes and is evicted, `get_status` returns `{ found: false }` even though the full call record with transcript is persisted to SQLite.
- **Solution**: add `getCallFromStore()` and `getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore()` standalone lookup functions. Use them **only** in read-only status surfaces: `voicecall.status` gateway method, `get_status` tool, legacy `status` mode, and CLI status command. `CallManager.getCall()` and `getCallByProviderCallId()` remain **active-only** to protect live webhook/realtime/auto-response paths from stale terminal records.
- **Architecture (per ClawSweeper review)**: store fallback is scoped to all three status read paths — NOT added to shared manager lookup methods. This prevents completed/evicted records from leaking into webhook stream acceptance, transcript handling, realtime resolution, and auto-response guards.
- **Latest-snapshot semantics**: `readCallRecordEvents()` returns oldest-first. The lookup functions iterate from the end to find the most recent snapshot when multiple persisted records exist for the same call.
- **What changed**: `manager/store.ts` — two exported lookup functions. `manager.ts` — public `callStorePath` getter. `index.ts` — store fallback in voicecall.status gateway + get_status tool + legacy mode. `cli.ts` — store fallback in CLI status. `manager.restore.test.ts` — 7 tests.
- **What did NOT change**: no schema, config, API, or provider changes. `getCall()`/`getCallByProviderCallId()` stay active-only.

**Maintainer checklist**

- Fix classification: Root-cause bug fix in voice-call status read paths
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High — all tests pass on upstream/main base

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
voice_call get_status returns { found: false } for completed calls once evicted from in-memory activeCalls map, despite full transcript persisted to SQLite. The fix scopes store fallback to status-only read paths.

**Real environment tested:**
- Platform: Linux x86_64
- Node.js: v22.22.0
- Runtime: Vitest v4.1.8
- Branch: fix/voice-call-store-fallback-96586 (2ca00be) — rebased on upstream/main

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```sh
node ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts extensions/voice-call/index.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**After-fix evidence:**
All tests pass including 7 new store-fallback tests:
- keeps getCall active-only: completed/evicted calls are not found via manager ✓
- keeps getCallByProviderCallId active-only: completed calls not found via manager ✓
- returns undefined for calls not in activeCalls or store ✓
- getCallFromStore returns completed call by callId ✓
- getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore returns completed call by providerCallId ✓
- getCallFromStore returns the latest (not oldest) snapshot ✓
- getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore returns the latest snapshot ✓

**Observed result after fix:**
- manager.getCall() / getCallByProviderCallId() — active-only ✓
- getCallFromStore() / getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore() — standalone, latest-snapshot ✓
- voicecall.status gateway method — store fallback for evicted calls ✓
- get_status tool — store fallback ✓
- Legacy status mode — store fallback ✓
- CLI status command — store fallback ✓

**What was not tested:**
- End-to-end with real Twilio/Telnyx provider
- Gateway restart + get_status integration flow

target.title: fix: resolve response prefix template variables in abort replies
target.body:
## Summary

Template variables are not populated when user issues early stop commands. 

- **Problem:** When a user sends a "stop" command (e.g. on WhatsApp), the abort reply displays unresolved template variables: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`
- **Why it matters:** The raw `{model}` and `{thinkingLevel}` placeholders leak into user-facing messages, looking broken and confusing.
- **What changed:** `createReplyPrefixContext()` now pre-seeds the `ResponsePrefixContext` with model, provider, and thinkingLevel resolved from config defaults, so early-exit paths like abort have valid values for template interpolation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to abort logic, dispatch flow, template resolution engine, or `formatAbortReplyText()`. The `onModelSelected()` callback still overwrites the defaults at runtime during normal replies.

## Change Type (select all)

- [X] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [X] Gateway / orchestration
- [X] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #45314

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Abort replies now display the resolved model name and thinking level from config (e.g. `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`) instead of literal `{model} - {thinkingLevel}` placeholders.
- Applies to all channels using a `responsePrefix` template with `{model}` or `{thinkingLevel}` variables.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / pnpm
- Model/provider: openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2.5
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp (reproduces on any channel with `responsePrefix` template)
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "messages": {
      "responsePrefix": "{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: "
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure a `responsePrefix` with `{model}` and/or `{thinkingLevel}` template variables
2. Start a conversation so the agent is processing
3. Send "stop" or `/stop`

### Expected

- Reply: `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

### Actual

- Reply: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

## Evidence
Before
![before](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/73faefe5-65c3-488d-a429-94edf51ca959)
After
![after](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0e70f899-9353-4679-a63a-4272a8d7d197)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `/stop` command triggers abort reply with resolved prefix; normal replies still resolve correctly after model selection overwrites defaults
- Edge cases checked: No model configured (falls back to hardcoded defaults); `onModelSelected` correctly overwrites pre-seeded values at runtime

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Incorrect model name or thinking level in reply prefix (would indicate config resolution mismatch)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `resolveDefaultModelForAgent()` or `resolveThinkingDefault()` could add overhead to context creation.
  - Mitigation: Both are lightweight synchronous config lookups with no I/O; already used elsewhere in the hot path.

---

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts` | Pre-seed `ResponsePrefixContext` with default model/provider/thinkingLevel from config |
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.test.ts` | New test: verifies pre-seeded context, `onModelSelected` override, and fallback defaults |


---

## Notes

- This PR is AI-assisted (fully tested: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` — 919 test files, 2642 tests passing).
- Add before/after screenshots showing the abort reply with unresolved vs resolved template variables.
- The root cause is that the abort fast-path in `dispatch-from-config.ts` exits before the agent run triggers `onModelSelected()`, so the prefix context never got model/thinkingLevel populated. The fix is entirely in context initialization — no changes to abort or dispatch logic.

target.title: fix(cost): estimate usage cost when API returns cost.total=0 but pricing is known
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

DeepSeek V4 API returns token usage but **no `cost` field** in completion responses. OpenClaw has known positive pricing configured, but the cost scan logic treats the missing/zero cost as fact — Control UI "Spend" shows ¥0.00 for all DeepSeek V4 usage.

**Root cause**: `scanTranscriptFile` only estimates when `costTotal === undefined`. When the API omits cost data (resulting in `cost.total: 0`), and pricing is known, the $0 is kept as-is.

**Fix**: Extend estimate condition to `costTotal === undefined || costTotal === 0`, plus bump `USAGE_COST_CACHE_VERSION` 4→5.

Closes #97047

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Infrastructure (cost/usage aggregation)

## Evidence

**Live API evidence** — DeepSeek V4 Flash response shows tokens but NO cost field:

```json
{
  "model": "deepseek-v4-flash",
  "usage": {
    "prompt_tokens": 5,
    "completion_tokens": 10,
    "total_tokens": 15
  }
}
```

No `cost` field in the API response. OpenClaw's transport layer records this as `cost: { total: 0 }` in the transcript.

**Before fix**: `cost.total: 0` + pricing known → skips estimate → **$0 accepted as fact**.

**After fix**: `cost.total: 0` + pricing known → `entry.costTotal === 0` triggers estimate → `estimateUsageCost` called → **cost computed from tokens × pricing**.

**Regression test**: DeepSeek V4 scenario (95029 input + 465 output, $0 API cost, known pricing ¥0.14/¥0.28 per 10K) verifies `totalCost > 0` and `missingCostEntries = 0`.

**Cache migration**: `USAGE_COST_CACHE_VERSION` 4→5 invalidates stale cached $0 entries for existing users.

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

target.title: fix: classify overloaded 429 responses as overloaded, not rate_limit (fixes #98101)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When a provider returns HTTP 429 with an "overloaded" body (e.g. z.ai code 1305: "The service may be temporarily overloaded, please try again later"), OpenClaw misclassifies it as a rate limit instead of an overload. This causes:

- Users see "⚠️ API rate limit reached" instead of the less alarming "temporarily overloaded" message
- The failover reason is `rate_limit` instead of `overloaded`, which triggers incorrect cooldown/rate-limit handling
- Users waste time debugging non-existent rate limits (rotating keys, waiting for limit resets)

The same class of bug was already fixed for billing 429 responses (#43447, #32828), but the overloaded case at 429 was missed.

## Why This Change Was Made

Two-level fix addressing both classification paths:

1. **Message classifier** (`classifyFailoverClassificationFromMessage`): `isRateLimitErrorMessage` matched the bare `429` pattern (`/rate[_ ]limit|too many requests|429/`) before `isOverloadedErrorMessage` could run. Reordered overloaded check before rate-limit check.

2. **HTTP status classifier** (`classifyFailoverClassificationFromHttpStatus`): The `status === 429` branch only checked billing then defaulted to `rate_limit`. Added an overloaded check matching the existing 503/499 pattern.

## User Impact

- Providers returning 429 + overloaded body now surface the correct "temporarily overloaded" message
- Failover reason correctly set to `overloaded` instead of `rate_limit`
- No change for genuine rate-limit 429 responses or billing 429 responses

## Evidence

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** HTTP 429 with overloaded body classified as `overloaded` instead of `rate_limit`
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node 24, OpenClaw 2026.5.28 local build from `upstream/main` (738b2be4b)
- **Steps run after the patch:**
  1. Built OpenClaw from the fix branch (`pnpm build`)
  2. Ran the failover classification test suite: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/failover-matches.test.ts`
  3. Verified the specific classification function with `classifyFailoverReasonFromHttpStatus(429, overloadedBody)` returns `"overloaded"`
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
✓  unit-fast  src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/failover-matches.test.ts (32 tests) 10ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  32 passed (32)
```
- **Observed result after fix:** All 32 tests pass including 3 new tests for 429 overloaded classification. The function `classifyFailoverReasonFromHttpStatus(429, overloadedMessage)` correctly returns `"overloaded"`, while `classifyFailoverReasonFromHttpStatus(429, rateLimitMessage)` still returns `"rate_limit"` and `classifyFailoverReasonFromHttpStatus(429, billingMessage)` returns `"billing"`.
- **What was not tested:** Live provider interaction with z.ai (requires real API key). The fix is validated at the classification function level which is the same code path used by the production CLI.

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)
